Introduction xlvii
(x) He explains the expression ^f:'1 as ^
•^T; It is suspected that this too might be another
instance of semantic variation. In his local dialect the expression
Srfigdra might have had the connotation of'chivalry or heroism,'
This line of argument on the part of the author reminds us of Bdm's
Sukandsopadeta.
We have already expressed our high appreciation of Utpalcfs
simple and graceful language. However, we cannot close our eyes
to some of the gross errors he has committed in grammatical cons-
truction, gender, compounds and interpretation : Srotriya is cons-
trued hy him Niyaminah—(observers of vows); Mdnyamdnapilr is
explained as Mdnya (worshipable) and Mdnapitrs or Pujakas, This
kind of interpretation sounds odd. He construes Akincandh (indigent)
as Akincitkardh puru$akdrahindh (LXVIII. 29). He explains the
word Slrimrtyufy (verse 61) as Stri tasya mdrqyati, which is evidently
wrong in grammar.
